FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), N=7,614 participants ages 10-13

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying social media variable: time on SM (hours per day) + Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ). Applies B1 (SM with own coefficient reported separately)

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression analysis (generalized additive mixed models with ROI and vertexwise analysis); medians/associations with β coefficients reported in Table 2 and Figure 1

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients reported for SM use and addiction across ROI regions in Table 2; standardized beta coefficients visualized in Figure 1; p-values and significance indicators provided

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis examining SM↔brain morphology association with numerical outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Primary publication in NeuroImage 2026; no evidence of preprint/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: SM is NOT the dependent variable only. Paper examines SM→brain structure (outcome), not demographics→SM. Thus condition (a) fails. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Cross-sectional design. Social media use measured as hours per day and SMAQ addiction scale. Main analyses adjusted for demographics, socioeconomic factors, genetic ancestry, non-SM screen time, scanner features.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, longitudinal data Year 2 (N=8,292; mean age 12.0 years 2018-2020) through Year 4

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying social media variable: social media time (Year 2). Applies B1 (SM with separate coefficient in mediation model)

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized structural equation modeling with mediation indirect effects testing; mediation pathway SM time → positive smoking expectancies → nicotine experimentation

FT_IC4.evidence: Mediation indirect effect (95% CI: 1.27%-10.67%, p=.013); association coefficient and confidence intervals reported in text and abstract

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis with prospective mediation structure examining SM exposure→outcome pathway

FT_IC6.evidence: Primary publication in The American Journal on Addictions 2026; no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: SM is NOT the dependent variable in all analyses. Paper examines SM (predictor)→nicotine experimentation (outcome). Condition (a) fails: paper has SM→non-SM-outcome analysis. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Prospective longitudinal design across 3 years (Year 2→Year 3→Year 4). Social media time at Year 2 predicts nicotine experimentation at Year 4; positive smoking expectancies at Year 3 mediate this association.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 5 (2021-2023), N=11,530 adolescents ages 13-16

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying social media variable: dating app use (online dating platform-specific use). Applies B1 (SM exposure with separate coefficient reported)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable linear regression with cluster-robust standard errors; dating app use coefficient (standardized β) reported with 95% CI and p-value

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized β=0.42 (95% CI 0.08-0.76, p=0.017) for association between dating app use and depression symptoms in adjusted model

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis examining dating app use↔depression symptoms association

FT_IC6.evidence: Primary publication in BMC Research Notes 2026; no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Dating app use is NOT the dependent variable. Paper examines dating app use (predictor)→depression symptoms (outcome). Condition (a) fails: paper has SM→non-SM-outcome analysis. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Cross-sectional design. Adjusted for sociodemographic covariates (age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental education) and study site. Low prevalence: 0.9% reported ever using dating app.
